other_observers: Steve Mlodinow; Mlodinow found the bird and immediately brought it to my attention.
description: The same size and shape as nearby "normal" (Common) Mallards. Fairly dark brown body contrasted with paler, grayer neck and head; demarcation between breast and neck obvious. Visible tertials on perched bird formed a somewhat paler, somewhat grayer panel, contrasting somewhat with overal brown body; this panel showed two faint white stripes. Bill fairly bright, uniform yellow, with small dark tip. Crown dark; thin transocular line dark. Neck with dark, very fine streaking. Rectrices dusky brown, contrasting little with rest of body; adult male Mallard standing nearby had much whiter rectrices. Feet deep orange-red. Speculum, seen well on bird with wings raised up on several occasions, was purplish-blue with a thin white trailing edge and a somewhat thicker, white leading edge.Behaviors: Feeding, preening, and resting along a muddy lake shore. Seemed indifferent to the presence of other ducks, neither avoiding them nor actively seeking them out; basically, just another bird in a loose flock of (Common) Mallards, Gadwalls, and 3 teal species. After about an hour's observation, the bird flew off to the east.Call: None heard.Plumage: basic definitive sensu Pyle
